title: 1 Corinthians 4
subtitle: The Apostles as Servants of Christ

heb.paragraph: The noun oikonomos (steward) in v. 1 designates the slave or freedperson entrusted with managing a household’s affairs. In the Greco-Roman world, the oikonomos was a position of significant responsibility but zero independent authority — everything was done on behalf of the master. Paul applies this to apostolic ministry: apostles are stewards of God’s mysteries, accountable to God alone. The Corinthians have no right to evaluate apostles by the criteria of rhetorical brilliance or social status.

The verb anakrinō (to examine/judge) appears four times in vv. 3–4, creating a concentrated focus on the theme of judgment. Paul insists he does not even judge himself (v. 3), let alone submit to Corinthian evaluation. Only the Lord’s eschatological judgment will render the final verdict. The term carries legal connotations: in Attic law, anakrisis was the preliminary examination before a trial. Paul is saying that the Corinthians are conducting a premature trial.

panels.ctx: Paul transitions from the general argument about divisions to the specific question of how apostles should be regarded. The stewardship metaphor draws on the widespread institution of household management in the Roman world. In Corinth, wealthy patrons managed their households through trusted stewards (often freedmen) who oversaw finances, slaves, and daily operations. Paul subverts the patronage system: instead of apostles being patrons whom believers serve, apostles are stewards whom God will judge. The phrase ‘it is required that those who have been given a trust must prove faithful’ (v. 2) uses the passive pistos heurethē, implying divine evaluation.

cross.note: Jesus’ parable of the faithful and unfaithful stewards provides the dominical background for Paul’s stewardship language.
Paul makes the same argument against judging another’s servant: ‘Who are you to judge someone else’s servant? To their own master, servants stand or fall.’
Paul affirms that all believers must appear before Christ’s judgment seat, extending the eschatological evaluation motif.

notes.note: The steward has two qualities: he is under authority (belonging to his master) and he must be faithful (pistos). The Corinthians were evaluating apostles by the wrong criteria — eloquence, social status, personal magnetism. The only criterion that matters is faithfulness to the trust committed.

Paul’s statement that he does not even judge himself is not false modesty but theological principle: human self-assessment is unreliable because the heart is deceitful (Jer 17:9). Only the Lord, at his coming, will ‘bring to light what is hidden in darkness and will expose the motives of the heart.’ Then each person will receive praise from God.

Calvin observes that Paul simultaneously elevates and humbles the apostolic office: apostles are servants of Christ (a high calling) but stewards of mysteries they did not create (a humble position). The faithful steward dispenses what belongs to another; he does not originate or modify the message.

Calvin warns against premature judgment in either direction — neither praising nor condemning ministers before the Lord’s verdict. He notes that Paul’s clear conscience does not equal innocence: only God’s searching examination can render final judgment.

The phrase hypēretas Christou (‘servants of Christ’) uses hypēretēs rather than doulos. The term originally meant ‘under-rower’ in a galley ship and denotes subordinate service. Paul chooses a term that emphasises functional subordination to Christ.
The phrase ho kairos (‘the time’) with the article refers to the eschatological moment of Christ’s return, not time in general. Paul consistently reserves final evaluation for the parousia.

Fee argues that the dual title — hypēretai (servants) and oikonomoi (stewards) — is deliberately chosen to contrast with the Corinthians’ view of apostles as competing sophists. Both terms denote subordination and accountability, the opposite of the autonomous authority the Corinthians were projecting onto their favourite teachers.

Fee stresses that Paul’s refusal to submit to human judgment is not arrogance but a consistent outworking of the cross-centred epistemology of chapters 1–2. If the cross overturns human wisdom, then human evaluative criteria are also overturned. Only the Lord’s eschatological light can reveal the truth.

Thiselton draws attention to the rhetorical shift: Paul uses houtōs (‘in this way’) to signal that he is now applying the general argument to the specific case. The audience should regard (logizesthō, ‘consider/reckon’) apostles not as patrons or rivals but as accountable stewards.

Thiselton notes that Paul’s triple disclaimer (‘I do not judge myself’ / ‘my conscience is clear’ / ‘that does not make me innocent’) demonstrates sophisticated awareness of the limitations of self-knowledge. The Lord alone has both the omniscience and the authority to render final verdict.

heb.gloss: to puff up / inflate / make arrogant
heb.paragraph: The verb physioō (to puff up/inflate) is a distinctively Corinthian term in Paul’s vocabulary: it appears seven times in 1 Corinthians (4:6, 18, 19; 5:2; 8:1; 13:4) and only once elsewhere in Paul (Col 2:18). The metaphor is visceral: the Corinthians are ‘inflated’ with self-importance like a balloon, filled with air rather than substance. Paul consistently contrasts being ‘puffed up’ with genuine love, which ‘does not boast, is not puffed up’ (13:4).

panels.ctx: Paul’s biting irony reaches its peak in this section. The phrase ‘already you have all you want! Already you have become rich! You have begun to reign — and that without us!’ (v. 8) is devastating sarcasm targeting a proto-‘realised eschatology’: some Corinthians apparently believed they had already entered the fullness of the eschatological kingdom. This over-realised eschatology — the conviction that the Spirit’s presence means complete spiritual triumph now — underlies many of the letter’s problems, from arrogance about spiritual gifts to denial of the future bodily resurrection (ch. 15). Against this triumphalism, Paul catalogues apostolic sufferings (vv. 9–13) that mirror the cross’s pattern.

cross.note: Paul’s extended catalogue of apostolic sufferings in the second Corinthian letter develops the same cruciform pattern.
Paul’s most detailed suffering catalogue, listing beatings, imprisonments, shipwrecks, and dangers of every kind.
Paul’s desire to know the ‘fellowship of sharing in Christ’s sufferings’ provides the theological rationale for the suffering catalogues.

notes.note: Paul’s statement ‘do not go beyond what is written’ is debated but likely refers to the scriptural quotations in chapters 1–3. The Corinthians should not elevate one leader over another beyond what Scripture warrants. The rhetorical questions of v. 7 — ‘What do you have that you did not receive?’ — demolish all grounds for boasting.

Paul’s sarcasm is withering: ‘Already you have all you want! Already you have become rich! You have begun to reign!’ The Corinthians were living as though the eschaton had arrived in full. Paul’s biting response: ‘How I wish that you really had begun to reign so that we also might reign with you!’

The apostolic peristasis catalogue (suffering list) presents a stark contrast to Corinthian triumphalism. Apostles are ‘on display at the end of the procession, like those condemned to die.’ The imagery is of the Roman triumphal procession, where prisoners of war were paraded last, destined for execution. This is the true apostolic condition: not power but suffering.

Calvin calls this ‘the most effective of all arguments for restraining arrogance.’ Everything the Corinthians possess — faith, gifts, knowledge — was received from God. To boast about received gifts is as absurd as a beggar boasting about alms.

Calvin notes the deliberate contrast between the Corinthians’ imagined royalty and the apostles’ actual condition. Paul uses irony not to humiliate but to awaken: if the Corinthians truly grasped the gospel, they would recognise that the apostolic pattern of suffering, not their pattern of self-satisfied triumphalism, is the norm for this age.

The phrase to mē hyper ha gegraptai (‘not beyond what is written’) is one of the most debated phrases in 1 Corinthians. Options include: (a) a general principle about Scripture, (b) a reference to specific OT passages quoted in chs. 1–3, or (c) a proverbial saying. The absence of a definite article before gegraptai suggests a formulaic expression.

The word theatron (‘spectacle’) refers to the Roman arena. Paul’s metaphor of apostles as condemned prisoners on public display draws on the vivid imagery of the amphitheatre, well known in Roman Corinth.

Fee argues that the Corinthians’ over-realised eschatology is the hermeneutical key to the entire letter. They believe they are already reigning with Christ, which leads to arrogance about gifts (chs. 12–14), indifference to bodily ethics (chs. 5–6), and denial of future resurrection (ch. 15). Paul’s response throughout is: ‘not yet.’

Fee notes that the peristasis catalogue functions not as self-pity but as a deliberate paradigm: apostolic suffering enacts the cross’s pattern. The Corinthians want power without suffering; Paul insists that the cross’s weakness is the shape of authentic Christian existence between the ages.

Thiselton identifies the rhetorical figure as eirōneia (irony): Paul adopts the Corinthians’ own language of satiety, wealth, and royal reign, then shatters it with the reality of apostolic suffering. The tricolon ‘already satisfied — already rich — already reigning’ mimics the structure of ancient acclamations.

Thiselton argues that the theatrical metaphor (theatron, v. 9) transforms the Corinthians from spectators of apostolic performance into witnesses of God’s paradoxical display of power-through-weakness. The catalogue reverses every Corinthian value: honour becomes dishonour, strength becomes weakness, eloquence becomes reviling.

sections.header: Verses 14–21 — "I Am Sending Timothy to You"

heb.paragraph: The noun paidagōgos in v. 15 refers to the household slave responsible for supervising a child’s daily conduct, accompanying them to school, and ensuring moral discipline. The paidagōgos was not the teacher but the caretaker. Paul draws the contrast: the Corinthians may have ‘ten thousand guardians in Christ’ (myrioi paidagōgoi) but only one father, because Paul personally brought them to birth through the gospel. The fatherhood claim establishes a unique relational authority that no subsequent teacher can replicate.

panels.ctx: Paul shifts from rebuke to paternal appeal. The father-children metaphor establishes Paul’s unique authority over the Corinthian community: he begot them through the gospel (v. 15). This is not merely emotional language but a claim rooted in the ancient household structure where the father’s authority was supreme (patria potestas in Roman law). Paul’s decision to send Timothy as his representative (v. 17) follows Greco-Roman epistolary convention, where an envoy carried the sender’s authority. The closing threat — ‘shall I come to you with a rod, or in love and with a gentle spirit?’ (v. 21) — employs the ancient rhetorical device of the dilemma (propositio), forcing the audience to choose repentance.

cross.note: Paul uses both maternal (‘nursing mother’) and paternal imagery to describe his relationship with the Thessalonian church.
Paul describes himself as ‘in the pains of childbirth’ until Christ is formed in the Galatians — the same generative metaphor.
Paul commends Timothy in similar terms: ‘I have no one else like him,’ sending him as his trusted representative.

notes.note: Paul distinguishes between guardians (paidagōgoi) and fathers. A guardian can instruct and discipline, but only a father begets. Paul founded the Corinthian church; he is their spiritual father. The implication: the Corinthians should imitate their father, not their guardians.

Timothy’s mission is to remind the Corinthians of Paul’s ‘way of life in Christ Jesus,’ which agrees with what Paul teaches ‘everywhere in every church.’ This is not local adaptation but universal apostolic practice. The Corinthians are not a special case exempt from the norms Paul establishes in all his churches.

Paul exposes the arrogance of those who assumed he would not return. His authority is not in impressive speech but in the kingdom’s power (dynamis). The closing dilemma — rod or love? — leaves the choice with the Corinthians. Their behaviour will determine the nature of his visit.

Calvin stresses that Paul’s fatherhood claim is grounded in the gospel, not in personal charisma. He begot them through the proclamation of Christ crucified. Therefore his call to ‘imitate me’ is not self-exaltation but an invitation to follow the cruciform pattern he embodies.

Calvin reads the contrast between ‘talk’ (logos) and ‘power’ (dynamis) as the definitive criterion for evaluating ministry: authentic authority is demonstrated not in eloquent speech but in the transformative power of the gospel to change lives.

The number myrioi (‘ten thousand’) is hyperbolic, denoting an indefinitely large number. The contrast is not between bad guardians and a good father but between many subsequent instructors and one irreplaceable parent.
The phrase en Christou Iēsou (‘in Christ Jesus’) modifies ‘my ways,’ not Timothy: Paul’s manner of life is shaped by his union with Christ, and this is what Timothy will model for the Corinthians.

Fee argues that Paul’s fatherhood claim is the personal application of the building metaphor: as the one who laid the foundation (3:10), Paul has a relationship to the Corinthian community that no subsequent teacher can claim. The sending of Timothy is not a substitute for Paul’s presence but a reminder of the cruciform lifestyle (vv. 9–13) that the Corinthians should be emulating.

Fee notes that the ‘arrogant’ (physiōthentes, ‘puffed up’) are specific individuals who will be named in chapter 5. Paul’s threat is not empty bluster but a genuine intention to exercise apostolic discipline on his next visit.

Thiselton highlights the speech-act force of ‘I became your father through the gospel’: this is a constitutive declaration that establishes a relationship, not merely a description of one. The imperative ‘be imitators of me’ (mimētai mou ginesthe) is grounded in this constitutive act.

Thiselton reads the kingdom-power contrast as an eschatological statement: the kingdom of God is not merely a matter of present talk (logos) but of future power (dynamis) already operative in the present through the Spirit. The rhetorical question forces a decision: repentance or confrontation.

themes.note: Mission peaks as Paul articulates his apostolic vocation as fatherhood, stewardship, and suffering witness. Judgment and Sovereignty score highly: the Lord alone evaluates stewards, and the arrogant will face divine power. Justice rises as Paul contrasts authentic and counterfeit ministry.

rows.label: vv.1–5
rows.text: Stewards Accountable to the Lord Alone

rows.label: vv.6–13
rows.text: Ironic Rebuke: Corinthian Triumphalism vs. Apostolic Suffering

rows.label: vv.14–21
rows.text: Paul’s Fatherly Authority and Timothy’s Mission

lit.note: Chapter 4 concludes the first major section of the letter (chs. 1–4) by moving from theological argument to personal application. Paul’s tone shifts from rebuke to appeal, from sarcasm to paternal tenderness, ending with a forced choice that sets up the disciplinary action of chapter 5.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: Calvin observes that Paul simultaneously elevates and humbles the apostolic office: apostles are servants of Christ (a high calling) but stewards of mysteries they did not create (a humble position)…

ppl.name: Timothy
ppl.role: Paul’s young protégé; co-author of six NT letters
ppl.text: Timothy is recruited at Lystra on the second journey (Acts 16:1–3) — the son of a Jewish mother and Greek father, circumcised by Paul to aid Jewish outreach. He becomes Paul’s most trusted emissary, sent to troubled churches when Paul cannot go. Paul twice calls him "my true son in the faith" and addresses two personal letters to him. He is co-author of six Pauline letters and appears at the end of Hebrews.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Apostles as Servants of Christ

translations.text: Now, brothers and sisters, I have applied these things to myself and Apollos for your benefit, so that you may learn from us the meaning of the saying, “Do not go beyond what is written.” Then you will not be puffed up in being a follower of one of us over against the other.

I have applied all these things to myself and Apollos for your benefit, brothers, that you may learn by us not to go beyond what is written, that none of you may be puffed up in favor of one against another.

For it seems to me that God has put us apostles on display at the end of the procession, like those condemned to die in the arena. We have been made a spectacle to the whole universe, to angels as well as to human beings.
For I think that God has exhibited us apostles as last of all, like men sentenced to death, because we have become a spectacle to the world, to angels, and to men.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The Apostles as Servants of Christ within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.
